chapters.title: How US meddling carved Korea in two
chapters.text:
Before the Second World War and the fall of the Japanese Empire, the Korean peninsula was a small Japanese colony with little global weight. Once the war ended, the United States and the Soviet Union both turned their eyes on this quiet stretch of land, each wanting to spread its own brand of politics. The US worried that the USSR might use Korea as a stepping stone toward Japan, so it offered the Soviets the northern half as a temporary trust and kept the southern half for itself.

The line drawn across the country fell on the thirty-eighth parallel, and the Korean people had no voice in the matter. They had gone from one foreign power, Japan, to two rival ones, the US and the USSR.

The split between North and South Korea was a stroke of the pen. Nothing in Korean history had marked that border as special.

In the north, Kim Il-sung rose to lead the new Democratic People's Republic of Korea. In the south, Syngman Rhee took charge of the Republic of Korea. Two years later, in 1950, both halves claimed the whole peninsula. That clash of claims lit the fuse for the Korean War. On June 25, Kim Il-sung launched a surprise attack on the south and seized Seoul, the capital. Soviet tanks rolled at the front of the invasion.

Several countries, including the United States and fifteen others, rushed to South Korea's defense. Together, they pushed Kim Il-sung's forces back. At the same time, Chinese troops moved in to support the North.

This summary opens a window onto life in North Korea through the eyes of people who fled the dictatorship. The aim is to help outsiders grasp the hardships of North Korean citizens and the walls that stand between them and the wider world.

chapters.title: A caste system underpins life in the North
chapters.text:
When the three-year Korean War ended, Kim Il-sung declared himself president and built a communist state. His rule rested on a balance of rewards and penalties. He gave special privileges to those he saw as loyal and punished anyone he suspected of disloyalty with a heavy hand. The tool he used was a caste system that sorted the population into three layers.

The core class sat at the top of the social pyramid. It included senior military officers, party members, and people who had proven their loyalty to Kim Il-sung during the civil war. Most of them lived in the capital.

The wavering class held people with good records who might still turn against the state. Members included Koreans who had come back from Japan and former members of the core class.

The hostile class included religious believers, Koreans with past ties to the south, and political suspects. Anyone convicted of a political crime from the core or wavering classes also dropped down here. Members of this group needed special permission to enter the capital, and they were barred from living in the better farmland of the south.

Your caste decided where you could live, how much schooling you and your children could reach for, and what jobs you could hold. Children inherited their parents' caste. There was no ladder to climb. You could only stay where you were or slide down.

Caste also shaped what you could eat. Food came from state distribution centers. You handed over money, and the government issued coupons for staples. Money alone did not get you far. Each caste had a minimum ration set for its members. To this day, most North Koreans do not even know that the caste system exists by name, since contact between groups is so thin. Suspicion runs deep as people bump into discrimination and dead ends in education and work.

The way any society splits its classes reflects how its wealth is shared.

chapters.title: The Juche doctrine keeps the regime in power
chapters.text:
When Kim Il-sung set up the communist state, he dreamed of a North Korea that stood on its own feet. To make that dream real, he poured the Juche philosophy into the minds of his citizens. The teaching said that only strong people could carry the banner of communism, and that North Korea could survive without foreign help if its people paid the price.

Juche lessons showed up in speeches and study sessions that everyone had to attend. Factory workers lost work hours to these lectures. They also had to write essays proving they had taken the lessons to heart. Juche also carried a strong cult of the leader, and the people were told to worship him. Any country that had given up communism was labelled a nation of weak citizens. North Korean propaganda pointed at South Korea as proof, calling it a country that had chosen democracy and a tight bond with the United States.

Once a belief system justifies tyranny, people learn to live with it.

Citizens were pushed to watch their neighbors and report anything suspicious to the authorities, which turned nearly everyone into a quiet spy for the state. The press loved to print stories of children turning in their own parents as proof of devotion. Suspicious acts included staying overnight at a relative's home without a permit, cooking on a hot plate, using a rice cooker, lighting a bulb stronger than forty watts, or burning through too much electricity.

Families were grouped into units called inminban. The leader of each unit, usually an older woman, had a government handler she could report to about odd speech, movements, or behavior. The state also set up a patrol group called the kyuch'aldae, which had the right to barge into homes without warning, search for contraband, and arrest anyone who broke the rules. Fear spread so wide that no one dared speak out. In time, Kim Il-sung controlled the whole country.

Unlike students in other communist states, North Korean students and intellectuals never dared to protest, as Barbara Demick notes.

chapters.title: Kim Il-sung wanted to be worshipped like a god
chapters.text:
Kim Il-sung wanted to be the only voice in his people's lives, so he banned Christianity and other religions. He cast himself as a godlike figure and his son, Kim Jong-il, as a Christ-like one. He hoped to spark awe and build a cult by borrowing the language of faith. Worship of the leader grew almost on its own, woven into newspapers, books, and songs that everyone absorbed without question. The state's media was his main tool. The press told stories about him and his son. One claim was that singing songs about Kim Il-sung could calm a stormy sea. Another held that the birth of Kim Jong-il had lit a bright star and a double rainbow in the sky.

Every home and office had to keep portraits of Kim Il-sung and Kim Jong-il on the wall. Public buildings did too, and the size of the portraits grew with the size of the room. The press praised citizens who had risked their lives to rescue the leaders' portraits from burning buildings. Children never got to celebrate their own birthdays. Each year on April 15 and February 16, the birthdays of the two leaders, they thanked the nation's fathers for the welfare they provided.

Leader birthdays were some of the few bright spots of the year. People could get meat and electricity, rare treats in daily North Korean life.

The cult grew so wide that both leaders were hailed as experts in every field. They would occasionally visit factories, offices, and farms and offer tips on the spot. The state treated those comments as expert advice and tried to put them into practice.

The peak of the cult came with the ten days of mourning for Kim Il-sung. He died of a heart attack on July 9, 1994. Thousands gathered at his statues and tried to outdo each other in their grief, wailing and even banging their heads against the walls. Anyone who could not produce tears feared for their safety. The state banned all entertainment during those ten days. A similar wave of mourning followed Kim Jong-il's death on December 17, 2011.

chapters.title: The fall of the USSR dragged North Korea down with it
chapters.text:
North Korea's economy leaned hard on its communist neighbors. Outwardly, Kim Il-sung kept preaching self-reliance. Inwardly, the country ran on cheap oil, electricity, and factory equipment shipped in from the USSR, China, and other allies. When the USSR collapsed in 1991, North Korea's economy sank with it. Its factories could not run, and there was nothing to sell abroad.

The state owed piles of debt to China and Russia. When the loans went unpaid, both countries cut off raw materials. The loss of power and fuel hit the population hard. The state rationed electricity down to almost nothing. Homes lost heating and running water. Many North Koreans died of cold and the illnesses that followed. The power shortage also broke the harvest, since farms depended on machinery and modern methods, and the country of nearly twenty-three million people ran out of food.

The more there was to complain about, the more important it was to make sure no one did, as Barbara Demick put it.

Foreign donors refused to step in. They felt the government had to fix its priorities first. A global panic broke out in March 1993 when North Korea pulled out of the treaty that stopped the spread of nuclear weapons. Many feared that a North Korean bomb could start a war, since the state poured huge sums into its military. The armed forces numbered about a million soldiers, an enormous army for a country its size.

The United States tried to trade energy aid for a freeze on the nuclear program, but the deal fell apart. North Korea did not stick to its side of the bargain. The people did not blame their own leaders for the darkness and hunger. They blamed the United States and the sanctions.

The state kept funding its nuclear program while its own people went without light and food.

A telling detail of daily life: in Pyongyang at night, it is almost impossible to tell one building from another, because the city is so dark.

chapters.title: Black markets filled the gap left by empty food centers
chapters.text:
The breakdown of the factories hit the economy hard, and a string of poor harvests finally broke its back. The state slid into a deep nutrition crisis. Once the distribution centers stopped stocking food, the country began a massive scramble for anything edible. Selling food and other goods went against the spirit of communism, and the authorities called it an economic crime. Kim Il-sung eased the rules so people could sell homegrown vegetables. That small opening did not solve anything, and people began to trade much more than vegetables.

As the official food network collapsed, the black market grew. People turned to illegal trade to keep themselves fed. Tofu, noodles, and kimchi were easy to find. Rice was rare because Kim Il-sung had banned the sale of grain. In 1997, the state made selling grain a capital offense. Ordinary people fell back on cornmeal as a substitute for rice.

The people swallowed their fate and never questioned the leadership. Each new hardship brought a fresh theory from the propaganda machine, designed to explain why the state could not share more. Officials claimed that food had to be held back so that on the day of reunification, the North could feed South Koreans who had been starving for years. On other days, the state blamed the United States and the sanctions for the famine.

The hunger became a strange badge of patriotism, tied to devotion to the country.

People started selling their homes when they could no longer pay the steep prices on the illegal food market. A black market in real estate rose up, where citizens swapped houses to survive.

Soon, families that had once lived in comfortable homes were out on the streets. They slept in train stations and begged for food, and many died of starvation. By the time Kim Jong-il took over after his father's death in 1994, the famine had already taken millions of lives.

chapters.title: Defection was the only way out for the desperate
chapters.text:
In North Korea, the pace of defection sped up after the famine. Before then, those who left were mainly diplomats sent abroad on official trips. Once that trickle turned into a stream, the state began holding the relatives of those diplomats hostage at home, to make sure the envoys came back to their families.

Even knowing that defection was a capital crime did not stop people from trying to reach the South. Most saw it as a better option than the hunger and homelessness at home. The route out ran through the northeastern Chinese border and across two rivers. The coast was fenced off, so boats were useless. The defectors had to cross on foot. Some who could not walk paid others to carry them across.

On the far side, they waited for guards to look away or bribed the ones on duty. Some hired brokers who planned the trip and handled the bribes. These smugglers also arranged fresh clothes, a place to sleep, food, and sometimes travel papers. A broker might line up a passport, often stolen and altered, and book a flight from China to South Korea for the right price. Anyone caught defecting or working as a broker faced severe punishment, including execution.

The hope of a better life on the other side pushed people to take the deadly risk.

Brokers also bought wives for unmarried Chinese men, and many North Korean women accepted the deal. More than half of the 100,000 North Koreans living in China were women, often enslaved and poorly treated by their new husbands.

Other smugglers moved Chinese electronics and DVDs into North Korea. When no one was watching, North Koreans secretly watched South Korean soap operas. The shows pulled them in. The actors spoke the same language and looked like them. People could see how others lived abroad, and the boldest wanted to see with their own eyes if it was real.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
For years, outside experts kept predicting that the North Korean regime would collapse. Against all odds, it survived. It outlived the fall of the Soviet Union, the death of Kim Il-sung, and the fall of the Berlin Wall. The state clings to its policy of militarism, building up arms and testing nuclear weapons in the open. In the leader's view, the world is full of enemies that threaten the country's safety. The economy is held back by weak and baseless reforms. In one strange rule, only women over forty are allowed to sell their own products. Everyone else has to work in state factories for small pay, and that is the best-case scenario. Some factories cannot even pay their workers at all.

It is easy for outsiders who have never lived under tyranny to judge North Koreans for putting up with a dictator. In truth, those who live under the regime have no real choice. They have no access to decent schools or to outside media that could show them a different life behind the wall that separates their country from the rest of the world. With constant propaganda, they only grow more attached to the life the state has built around them.

For anyone planning to visit, the country does have a small number of tourist sites that draw visitors. A few simple tips help. You will be assigned a guide, and it is wise to keep them in sight at all times for your own safety. Stay inside the official tourism areas. Be ready to stay off social media and the internet for the whole visit. Following these rules helps keep a strange and tightly watched trip on track.
